text: Altitude training significantly increases hemoglobin levels compared to low-altitude training.
Altitude training increases hemoglobin mass compared to low-altitude training, though the effect is moderate.
Altitude training has no significant effect on maximal oxygen uptake (VO2max) compared to low-altitude training.
Altitude training improves time-trial performance compared to low-altitude training.
The 'live high, train high' (LHTH) approach is more effective than other altitude training modalities for enhancing aerobic capacity.
Altitude training interventions lasting longer than three weeks are more effective than shorter interventions for improving aerobic capacity.
Altitude training improves aerobic capacity primarily through hematological adaptations rather than through increases in VO2max.
